summary: Fowler recounts an apocryphal story about an outsourced project where a consultancy made a show of comprehensive JUnit testing, only for the tests to contain no actual assertions. The team achieved 100% code coverage while validating nothing meaningful. The post serves as a cautionary tale about gaming testing metrics and the importance of understanding what code coverage actually measures. Fowler notes that even assertion-free tests aren't entirely useless since they can catch runtime exceptions, but the practice represents a fundamental misunderstanding or deliberate subversion of testing's purpose.
key_points: Tests without assertions can achieve 100% code coverage while proving nothing about correctness
Impressive testing metrics can be gamed, making them unreliable quality indicators on their own
Code coverage data requires careful interpretation - high coverage doesn't guarantee meaningful tests
Even assertion-free tests have minimal value by catching runtime exceptions like null pointers
money_quotes: They proudly showed the client all the tests and the green bar.
However there weren't any assertions in the JUnit tests.
You can do this and have 100% code coverage - which is one reason why you have to be careful on interpreting code coverage data.
Although assertion-free testing is mostly a joke, it isn't entirely useless.
key_insight: Code coverage metrics without examination of assertion quality can create an illusion of thorough testing while validating nothing.
